summary: Engineering task plan for the 1985-NY-Yankees Azure Function project. The system queries GPT-4o via TRAPI for the 1985 New York Yankees roster and stores results to Azure Blob Storage, triggered nightly via an Azure Functions Timer Trigger. Authentication uses Managed Identity with no API keys. Infrastructure is provisioned via Bicep on Azure. Partial implementation already exists (trapi_client.py, blob_writer.py, function_app.py, tests/, /infra Bicep modules).
assumptions: Python 3.11 is the runtime for the Azure Function on a Linux Consumption plan.
TRAPI (Azure OpenAI proxy) is accessible via an internal endpoint using DefaultAzureCredential bearer tokens.
GPT-4o deployment name is 'gpt-4o' and API version is '2024-02-01'.
Azure Blob Storage container is named 'yankees-roster'.
Managed Identity (system-assigned) is used for all Azure resource access — no API keys or connection strings.
GitHub Actions is used for CI/CD.
Bicep is used for Infrastructure as Code under /infra.
Application Insights is used for observability and logging.
Partial implementation already exists: trapi_client.py, blob_writer.py, function_app.py, and tests/ are present in the repo.
Infra Bicep modules for storage, functionapp, and rbac are already scaffolded under /infra.
Timer Trigger cron schedule targets nightly execution (e.g., 0 0 2 * * * UTC).
Resources tagged with project=1985-NY-Yankees and owner=rciapala.
open_questions: What is the exact nightly execution time (UTC) for the Timer Trigger?
What is the exact TRAPI endpoint URL and required prompt format for retrieving the 1985 Yankees roster?
Should the blob output be JSON, CSV, or plain text?
What is the blob file naming convention — static filename (roster.json) or timestamped?
Should previous roster blobs be overwritten or versioned/appended?
Are there retry policies required if TRAPI or Blob Storage calls fail transiently?
What Application Insights workspace and resource group should be targeted?
Is there a required minimum test coverage threshold?
Which Azure subscription and resource group should be used for deployment?

waves.name: Foundation
waves.objective: Establish project approval, provisioned infrastructure, managed identity RBAC, and CI/CD pipeline as the base for all implementation work.
entry_criteria: spec.md reviewed and understood by the engineering team
Azure subscription and resource group available
GitHub repository access confirmed for all contributors
TRAPI endpoint and deployment details confirmed
exit_criteria: Azure Storage Account and Blob container provisioned (INF-01 complete)
Azure Function App provisioned with system-assigned managed identity (INF-02 complete)
RBAC roles granted to managed identity on storage account (SEC-01 complete)
GitHub Actions CI/CD pipeline configured and functional (REL-01 complete)

waves.name: Core Build
waves.objective: Implement and locally validate the TRAPI client, blob writer, nightly Timer Trigger, and runtime configuration.
entry_criteria: Azure Storage container accessible via managed identity
TRAPI endpoint and prompt format confirmed
exit_criteria: TRAPI client retrieves 1985 Yankees roster from GPT-4o (APP-01 complete)
Blob writer stores roster to 'yankees-roster' container (APP-02 complete)
Timer Trigger function orchestrates APP-01 and APP-02 on nightly schedule (APP-03 complete)
All required runtime app settings configured in Function App (CFG-01 complete)

waves.name: Hardening
waves.objective: Validate security posture, add observability and alerting, implement retry/error handling, and achieve unit test coverage targets.
entry_criteria: Core Build wave exit criteria met
Function App deployed and reachable in Azure
exit_criteria: No API keys or secrets present in code or config (SEC-02 complete)
Application Insights connected with structured logging and alert rule (OBS-01 complete)
Retry and error handling implemented and unit-tested (REL-02 complete)
All unit tests passing with ≥80% coverage (QA-01 complete)

waves.name: Release Readiness
waves.objective: Validate end-to-end nightly execution in production, confirm monitoring is active, and complete operational documentation.
entry_criteria: All tests passing in CI pipeline
Production deployment completed via CD pipeline
exit_criteria: End-to-end nightly run validated in production (QA-02 complete)
Monitoring and alerting active and verified (OBS-02 complete)
readme.md and operational documentation complete (DOC-01 complete)
All success criteria from spec.md met

tasks.title: Review and approve implementation task plan
tasks.description: Review this engineering task plan against spec.md requirements and approve it before implementation begins. Confirm all open questions are addressed or assigned owners, and validate assumptions with stakeholders.
outputs: Approved task plan
Resolved or owner-assigned open questions list
GitHub issues created for all tasks
acceptance_criteria: Task plan reviewed by engineering lead and team
All open questions documented with owners or resolved prior to Core Build wave start
Plan approved and communicated to team
GitHub issues created for all tasks with correct labels and wave assignments
risks: Unresolved open questions (TRAPI endpoint, blob format, schedule time) may block Core Build wave

tasks.title: Provision Azure Storage Account and Blob Container via Bicep
tasks.description: Provision an Azure Storage Account and a Blob container named 'yankees-roster' using Bicep IaC under /infra/modules/storage.bicep. Configure identity-based access (AzureWebJobsStorage__accountName) with no connection strings or keys. Tag resources with project=1985-NY-Yankees and owner=rciapala.
outputs: Provisioned Azure Storage Account
Blob container 'yankees-roster' created
Storage account name output from Bicep deployment
acceptance_criteria: Storage Account exists in the target resource group
Blob container 'yankees-roster' is created and accessible
No storage account keys used — AzureWebJobsStorage__accountName pattern only
Resources tagged with project=1985-NY-Yankees and owner=rciapala
Bicep deployment completes without errors and outputs storage account name
risks: Storage Account name must be globally unique — use a parameterized or uniqueString-derived name in Bicep

tasks.title: Provision Azure Function App with Linux Consumption Plan and Managed Identity
tasks.description: Provision an Azure Function App using a Linux Consumption plan with Python 3.11 runtime via infra/modules/functionapp.bicep. Enable system-assigned managed identity and configure all required app settings. Tag resources appropriately.
outputs: Provisioned Azure Function App
System-assigned managed identity enabled with principal ID output
Function App app settings configured
acceptance_criteria: Function App deployed on Linux Consumption plan (kind: functionapp,linux, reserved: true)
Python 3.11 runtime set (linuxFxVersion: Python|3.11)
System-assigned managed identity enabled and principal ID is available for RBAC assignment
AzureWebJobsStorage__accountName set (not AzureWebJobsStorage connection string)
STORAGE_ACCOUNT_NAME, TRAPI_ENDPOINT, TRAPI_DEPLOYMENT_NAME, TRAPI_API_VERSION app settings present
Function App health check returns HTTP 200
risks: Consumption plan cold-start adds latency to nightly execution — acceptable for this batch workload but should be noted in docs

tasks.title: Configure RBAC roles for Managed Identity on Storage Account
tasks.description: Grant the Function App's system-assigned managed identity Storage Blob Data Owner and Storage Queue Data Contributor roles on the storage account via infra/modules/rbac.bicep. Confirm TRAPI access uses DefaultAzureCredential bearer token with no stored credentials.
outputs: RBAC role assignments deployed via Bicep
Managed identity confirmed capable of reading/writing blobs and queue operations
acceptance_criteria: Storage Blob Data Owner (or Contributor) role assigned to managed identity on storage account
Storage Queue Data Contributor role assigned to managed identity for AzureWebJobsStorage trigger support
No storage account keys, API keys, or connection strings in any source or config file
TRAPI client authenticates via DefaultAzureCredential bearer token — confirmed in code review
RBAC assignments deployed via Bicep and verifiable in Azure portal IAM blade
risks: RBAC role propagation takes ~5 minutes after assignment — initial auth failures may occur immediately post-deploy

tasks.title: Configure GitHub Actions CI/CD pipeline for build, test, and deploy
tasks.description: Set up or validate GitHub Actions workflows: a CI workflow to install dependencies and run pytest on pull requests, and a CD workflow to deploy the Function App to Azure on merge to main. Use OIDC federation or GitHub Secrets for Azure login — no hardcoded credentials.
outputs: CI workflow running pytest on PRs
CD workflow deploying to Azure Function App on merge to main
acceptance_criteria: CI workflow triggers on pull_request and runs pytest with coverage reporting
CD workflow triggers on push to main and deploys Function App zip package to Azure
No secrets hardcoded in workflow YAML — use GitHub environment secrets or OIDC
Deployment completes without errors and Function App reflects updated code
CI status badge added to readme.md
risks: OIDC federation requires Azure AD federated credential setup — ensure permissions are available in the target tenant

tasks.title: Implement TRAPI client to query GPT-4o for 1985 Yankees roster
tasks.description: Implement or validate trapi_client.py to send a structured prompt to GPT-4o via the TRAPI Azure OpenAI endpoint and return a parsed list of 1985 New York Yankees players. Authentication must use DefaultAzureCredential bearer token — no API keys.
outputs: trapi_client.py with get_roster() or equivalent function returning structured player list
acceptance_criteria: trapi_client.py sends a prompt to TRAPI endpoint requesting the 1985 Yankees roster
Authentication uses DefaultAzureCredential — no API keys present in code or configuration
Response is parsed and returns a list of player names (and optionally positions/jersey numbers)
HTTP errors (4xx, 5xx) are caught and raise meaningful exceptions with context
Unit tests exist covering successful response parsing and HTTP error handling
risks: GPT-4o response format is non-deterministic — prompt must be engineered for consistent structured output (e.g., JSON list)

tasks.title: Implement blob writer to persist roster to Azure Blob Storage
tasks.description: Implement or validate blob_writer.py to serialize the roster data (JSON or defined format) and write it to the 'yankees-roster' container using DefaultAzureCredential. Define and document blob naming convention and overwrite behavior.
outputs: blob_writer.py with write_roster() or equivalent function
Blob naming convention documented
acceptance_criteria: blob_writer.py writes roster data to 'yankees-roster' container in Azure Blob Storage
Authentication uses DefaultAzureCredential via BlobServiceClient — no connection strings or keys
Blob name convention is defined and consistently applied (e.g., roster.json or roster-YYYYMMDD.json)
Overwrite behavior is explicitly defined and implemented
Unit tests cover write success and storage SDK error scenarios using mocked BlobServiceClient
risks: If overwrite=False and blob exists, write may raise an error — define overwrite policy explicitly

tasks.title: Implement Azure Function Timer Trigger for nightly roster pipeline
tasks.description: Implement or validate function_app.py with a Timer Trigger (cron schedule for nightly execution) that calls the TRAPI client to retrieve the roster and the blob writer to persist it. Include structured logging at each step.
outputs: function_app.py with nightly Timer Trigger orchestrating APP-01 and APP-02
acceptance_criteria: Timer Trigger cron expression targets nightly execution and confirmed with team (e.g., 0 0 2 * * *)
Function calls trapi_client to retrieve roster and blob_writer to persist it in sequence
Structured log messages emitted for: function start, TRAPI call initiation, roster received (player count), blob write, and function completion
Exceptions are logged with context and re-raised (not swallowed) to trigger alerting
Local test run (func start --verbose) executes successfully end-to-end
risks: Consumption plan cold-start on first nightly run adds latency; acceptable for batch workload

tasks.title: Configure and validate all runtime app settings for Function App
tasks.description: Ensure all required app settings (STORAGE_ACCOUNT_NAME, TRAPI_ENDPOINT, TRAPI_DEPLOYMENT_NAME, TRAPI_API_VERSION, AzureWebJobsStorage__accountName) are present in the Azure Function App configuration. Validate no settings are hardcoded in source files.
outputs: Azure Function App app settings validated and complete
local.settings.json.example updated to reflect all required settings
acceptance_criteria: All required environment variables present in Function App configuration in Azure portal
No endpoint URLs, account names, or identifiers hardcoded in source files
local.settings.json.example is complete, accurate, and includes all required keys
local.settings.json (with real values) is confirmed in .gitignore and not tracked by git
Function runs locally using local.settings.json without errors
risks: Missing or misconfigured app settings cause silent runtime failures — validate before first nightly run

tasks.title: Security audit: validate no API keys or secrets in code or configuration
tasks.description: Audit all source files, configuration files, and GitHub Actions workflows to confirm no API keys, connection strings, passwords, or secrets are present. Validate all Azure SDK clients use DefaultAzureCredential. Verify .gitignore covers local.settings.json.
outputs: Security audit findings documented
All secrets confirmed externalized to Azure App Settings or GitHub Secrets
acceptance_criteria: No API keys, passwords, or connection strings found in any tracked source file
local.settings.json is listed in .gitignore and not present in git history
All Azure SDK clients (BlobServiceClient, credential) instantiated with DefaultAzureCredential
GitHub Actions workflows use OIDC or GitHub Secrets — no hardcoded credentials in YAML
Secret scanning (GitHub Advanced Security or equivalent) reports clean
risks: Developer may have accidentally committed local.settings.json — verify git log and history

tasks.title: Configure Application Insights for structured logging and failure alerting
tasks.description: Provision Application Insights (via Bicep if not already present) and connect it to the Azure Function App. Configure structured logging for function execution, TRAPI calls, and blob writes. Create an alert rule for function execution failures.
outputs: Application Insights connected to Function App
Alert rule for function invocation failures active
Log query for roster write events documented
acceptance_criteria: Application Insights resource provisioned and APPLICATIONINSIGHTS_CONNECTION_STRING set in Function App
Function execution traces visible in Application Insights Logs (customEvents or traces table)
Alert rule created: fires when function exceptions > 0 in a 24-hour window
Alert notification channel configured (email or webhook) and recipient list confirmed
Log sampling settings in host.json tuned to avoid dropping critical exception traces
risks: host.json samplingSettings excludedTypes may need tuning to retain exception logs; over-sampling can incur cost

tasks.title: Implement retry and error handling for TRAPI and Blob Storage calls
tasks.description: Add retry logic with exponential backoff to trapi_client.py for HTTP 429 and 5xx responses, and transient retry to blob_writer.py. Ensure the Timer Trigger function propagates exceptions rather than swallowing them, enabling Application Insights alerting.
outputs: Retry logic in trapi_client and blob_writer
Exception propagation confirmed in function_app
acceptance_criteria: TRAPI client retries on HTTP 429 and 5xx with exponential backoff (max 3 retries)
Blob writer retries on transient Azure Storage errors (network timeouts, ServiceUnavailable)
Unrecoverable errors logged with full context (endpoint, error code, message) and re-raised
function_app.py does not catch and suppress exceptions that should trigger Application Insights alerts
Retry behavior unit-tested with mock HTTP responses (429, 500, success-after-retry)
risks: TRAPI rate limits on a nightly schedule are unlikely but should be documented; excessive retry on a fixed schedule is low risk

tasks.title: Write and validate unit tests for all application modules
tasks.description: Ensure comprehensive pytest unit tests exist for trapi_client.py, blob_writer.py, and function_app.py using mocked external dependencies. Run with pytest-cov and verify coverage meets the ≥80% threshold across all modules.
outputs: All tests passing
Coverage report with ≥80% line coverage
CI pipeline running tests on every PR
acceptance_criteria: All pytest tests pass with zero failures or errors
pytest-cov coverage report shows ≥80% line coverage across trapi_client, blob_writer, and function_app
TRAPI client tests mock HTTP calls (requests or httpx) and cover success, 429, and 5xx error paths
Blob writer tests mock BlobServiceClient and cover write success and transient error with retry
Function app tests mock trapi_client and blob_writer and validate orchestration and exception propagation
CI pipeline executes tests on every pull request and blocks merge on failure
risks: Azure SDK mocking requires careful patching of BlobServiceClient constructors — incorrect patch paths cause tests to hit live Azure

tasks.title: Validate end-to-end nightly execution in production
tasks.description: Trigger the Azure Function manually in production (or wait for first scheduled nightly run). Verify the 1985 Yankees roster is retrieved from TRAPI GPT-4o and stored as a blob in the 'yankees-roster' container. Confirm via Application Insights logs.
outputs: Validated roster blob present in Azure Storage
End-to-end execution confirmed with Application Insights trace
Spot-check of roster data against known 1985 Yankees roster
acceptance_criteria: Function invocation completes successfully (no exceptions in Application Insights)
Blob containing 1985 Yankees roster exists in 'yankees-roster' container post-execution
Roster data passes spot-check against known 1985 Yankees players (e.g., Don Mattingly, Rickey Henderson)
Function execution duration is within acceptable bounds (< 60 seconds)
Second triggered run produces correct overwrite or versioned blob per defined naming convention
risks: First production run may fail due to RBAC propagation delay or misconfigured app settings — wait 10 minutes post-deploy before triggering

tasks.title: Validate live monitoring and alerting in production
tasks.description: After production deployment, confirm Application Insights captures nightly function execution with structured logs, blob write events are traceable, and failure alert rules fire correctly when injecting a test failure.
outputs: Confirmed live monitoring with at least one successful execution trace
Validated alert fires on injected failure
Log query documented in readme
acceptance_criteria: At least one successful nightly execution trace visible in Application Insights Logs
Blob write event logged with player count and blob name
Alert rule tested by injecting a failure — notification received on configured channel
Log retention period confirmed (minimum 30 days) and documented
Monitoring and alerting summary added to readme.md or operations runbook
risks: Alert notification channel (email/Teams) must be configured with correct recipients before go-live — confirm ahead of first nightly run

tasks.title: Complete readme and operational documentation
tasks.description: Update readme.md with project overview, local development setup instructions, deployment guide, environment variables reference, monitoring notes, and a CI status badge. Ensure local.settings.json.example is accurate and complete.
outputs: Updated readme.md with all required sections
Accurate local.settings.json.example
acceptance_criteria: readme.md includes: project overview, prerequisites, local dev setup steps, deployment instructions, and environment variables reference
All environment variables documented with descriptions and placeholder example values
Monitoring and alerting section added to readme with Application Insights log query example
local.settings.json.example reflects all required settings with up-to-date keys
GitHub Actions CI status badge added to top of readme.md
